sentences.text: Sure, the leader.
Well, all of a sudden, we were in, in Antwerp.
And we were wondering what was going to happen then.
And that's when they announced then that we would be divided up.
Some would go to France.
Some would go to Holland.
Some would go to Belgium.
And some would go to England.
And I said, I wonder what's going to happen to us?
I wonder if we're going to be as a family together?
And that's when my mother told us that we're going to England.
And I said goodbye to some of the kids, other kids that I knew.
Because they all ended up going-- stay on the continent.
And then we crossed over from-- we, we never got off the ship.
We never got on land, is maybe what I should say.
And they had a gangplank going from, from our ship to this converted freighter, The [INAUDIBLE].
And we went on that.
And I was absolutely floored as to our living conditions on this ship.
We lived-- my mother made me stay with her, with the women.
There was segregation, right.
One hold, they had built bunk beds.
They weren't beds, really.
They were just wooden posts with, with boards in between.
And you could sleep there.
We weren't-- we weren't on the ship very long.
The, the toilet facilities were absolutely terrible.
They had outhouses off the back of the stern of the ship.
The food was, was atrocious.
It was just a terrible ship, terrible voyage.
They did take some of them people.
And we stopped in France.
And they let off some of the people.
I believe it was Le Havre.
I'm not positive, though.
I know it was in France.
And then we went to England.
So we may have been on that ship maybe three days and two nights.
But it was, compared to the St.
Louis, night and day.
I believe with Southampton.
No.
But I remember going into London.
We got into London.
And I remember, we got off the train.
And there was all this red and white bunting.
And, and people were standing around, milling around.
And I couldn't-- I thought, gee, these people were here to welcome us, which turned out to be completely false.
Because they, they had visiting royalty coming.
But it was a nice feeling that, you know, that we didn't see the swastika and that we saw red, red, white, and blue bunting.
And people seemed to be much happier.
They had didn't have this dour look about them that you-- we saw in Germany.
No.
It was so long ago.
There are some people-- my sister has a friend in Toronto who was aboard the ship.
He's, he's about eight years older than myself.
He said he remembers me.
But I don't recall him, Eric Spitz.
And-- but he said he remembers me.
And then there was another gentleman from Florida who said he remembers me.
But I don't remember him at all.
I don't remember any of these people.
Well, I remember, on the ship, on The [INAUDIBLE], my mother was talking to this one gentleman.
And he sort of yelled across, saying goodbye to some of the people who were still on the, on the St.
Louis.
They hadn't taken all those people off.
And-- but I don't remember anything much than that.
Well, then we were-- we, we went to this office, to this building.
And then we were assigned lodging.
And we went to this-- oh, it's like a boarding house.
And we stayed there.
And then, then the war started.
Now this-- we got there in July.
So in, in September the war started.
And then we were sent to a little town called Yelverton.
And the reason why we were moved out of London is because we were enemy aliens.
So we had to go-- it's north of Plymouth.
Now, we weren't-- we were not interned, but we were.
In other words, we, we had our travel restrictions put on us.
And we stayed in this hotel.
And we had to get specific permission to go to Plymouth.
But it was-- and we-- there wasn't much to do.
We walked around in, in the moor.
We did that.
And we picked berries.
And we stayed in the hotel.
And in the middle of the-- they had like a, oh, a village square.
And they had wild horses there.
And I remember my sister getting kicked by the horse.
And the horses were just all over the place.
But we didn't really do much.
I then got sent to school, because my mother wanted me to get some education.
So I went to the school to learn to read and write.
My mother tried to teach me to read and write.
She could, she could speak a little bit of English and-- from, from her school days.
But I really had to learn something.
Because I couldn't speak any English and couldn't even read.
So I was already eight years old.
Couldn't read German either.
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
And when we got-- when we got to England, before we got to Yelverton, I remember we stayed-- we then got out of the boarding house.
And we, we were put up with this family.
And the man, the man of the house of this family had a library of World War One books.
And I was absolutely fascinated by this collection of picture books of World War One.
And I asked if I could walk-- look through the books.
And they said yes.
Now I-- remember, I couldn't read a word of English.
And I was looking.
And I remember sitting on the floor, looking at this huge collection-- maybe 24 books-- of, of the British army in World War One.
So that fascinated me.
And I guess one of the things I was looking for was maybe to see a picture of my father.
But naturally, that was ridiculous.
Well, I knew that.
But I didn't feel any animosity toward the British.
Because after all, here I am, sitting in British soil in a British former soldier's home, looking at a history of the, of the British army in World War One.
And I was intrigued by the-- I've always been intrigued by the military.
So this was just another chapter in, in, in, in learning things about the military.
No.
No.
No.
And then we were issued gas masks.
And we were-- they had dug trenches, like bomb shelters, in the park.
I can recall that.
And I saw the bomb dirigibles, these bomb-- to prevent the bombers from low flying-- from flying too low.
They would get tangled up in these-- in the cables.
Yes.
But they had made preparations prior to that, like in August.
And maybe we left a little bit after the war was declared.
I'm sure it wasn't immediately.
But they, they decided we had to get out of, out of there.
Because we were enemy aliens.
And I-- you know, they didn't know that-- what we would do or the fact that we were persecuted Germans that we really weren't Germans.
Because they took away our citizenship.
No.
Well, I do remember some, some planes flying over.
And, and they, there were, there was talk that they were German planes.
But I don't recall any bombings whatsoever, no explosions, nothing.
But I do remember them having very, very many air raid warnings.
Because when I came to this country and that evening-- we landed New-- Christmas Eve.
And the first time I heard an American train, I dove under the bed.
Because I thought it was an air raid.
Because it sounded very similar to the British air raid system.
I remember that.
But as far as any bombings, no.
I think that all started after we left in 1940.
Well, we were very close.
We, we, we lived in this hotel.
And we had two rooms.
And we had-- in each room was a, a stove to keep-- to heat the room.
We ate downstairs, relatively meager rations by comparison with the early days of the St.
Louis.
But-- and as I said, we went out and collected berries, blueberries.
It wasn't bad.
We-- I remember we went to the tea house.
There was a little tea house.
And my mother went and we had tea there.
We spent a lot of time together, just walking and talking about all kinds of things, what were going to do?
And my mother kept saying, we're going to go to America.
We're going to be OK.
It's going to turn out fine.
Do you remember when you found out that you could go to America, that your papers had come through?
That would have been early December.
Then they notified us that our number was up.
And we were leaving on this-- the ship to Southampton, which was-- I mean, the Stockholm from Southampton.
And that was a Swedish ship.
And I remember the thing was all lit up.
And it had Sweden written on the side of it so that the U-boats would not, the German U-boats would not attack it.
Was-- it was neutral.
Throughout the whole war, yeah.
And there were many, many children on board.
But as far as the voyage itself, I remember nothing about that voyage.
It's one huge blank.
I guess it just didn't mean much to me at that time.
And I don't know.
It's interesting.
No, I don't remember that, either.
I remember leaving, leaving Yelverton.
But how we-- wait a minute-- somewhere along the line, we were in, in, in London.
And we stayed at a hotel.
Now, I don't remember when that was, whether that was coming or going.
I don't recall that there was.
I mean, I, I-- if there was, there would have been.
I think they had suffered so much as far as, as disappointment was concerned that they weren't going to celebrate anything until the-- their feet touched American soil.
They didn't say too much.
My mother, at that time, was, was really very sad and had gone through so much.
And you know, from then on, her, her whole life was, was very sad.
She never really recovered.
And my grandmother was, if I, if I can say this, was a professional mourner.
My, my grandfather died in 1925.
And she always dressed in dark clothes and always mourned his, his passing.
And he died of natural causes, I mean.
Yeah.
She had to be cajoled, yes.
She was-- she never really wanted to leave Germany.
She had no idea what she was going to do in this country.
She had-- she said she was too old to start all over again.
She felt-- and, and her brothers insisted that they-- that we stay.
They said, what would you do there?
You don't have a means of-- you have no training.
You have no education.
You have nothing to-- so you can earn a living.
You really should stay.
My mother said, absolutely not.
She, she said, we have got to get out of here.
She wanted to leave Germany many years before.
As soon as Hitler came to power, '33, '34, she wanted to get out of there.
And she pressed my father for going.
But he was a such a strong German nationalist that he had no desire to leave.
He felt his service to the fatherland would surmount any obstacles so that they would be left alone, which wasn't the case.
Yeah.
Oh, absolutely.
It was an, it was-- I couldn't believe that when we landed in, in the port of New York, which was Weehawken, lights were all over the place.
Now remember, I-- when-- I'm sure that it was because the contrast was so great.
There were no lights in, in Southampton.
It was, you know, pitch black.
And everything was on wartime footing.
And we arrive in this country.
And the place is lit up.
And I remember them checking our baggage.
They, they-- and they got rid of us off the ship very, very fast.
And down below, on the, on the dock, my uncle was waiting and hustled off-- hustled us through.
They hustled us through the, the immigration part.
And within a very short time, we were in the car.
And he was taking us home.
And when we got home, we had a Christmas tree waiting for us.
And my uncle's best friend, Sam [?
Reich, ?] was there acting as Santa Claus.
And we got presents.
And we were really made to feel welcome that we, we were here.
And it was a joyous occasion.
That is correct.
Not really.
Not really.
My family is not very emotional.
The only time I really recall my mother being extremely emotional is when my father passed away in 1937 and then when her second husband died.
Because she said it brought back so many memories of her first husband.
But they weren't-- I don't recall them being very emotional, no.
Eight.
Eight and a 1/2.
Yes.
My mother gets a job in, in New York City, working in the garment industry, sewing collars on t-shirts for 25 cents an hour.
My grandmother becomes a cleaning lady.
And I stay in Hackensack, New Jersey, with my uncle, who-- I become his ward.
In other words, he becomes my guardian, adopts me.
And I stayed in that household-- well, he left for World War II as soon as that broke out.
And I stayed in Hackensack and lived with my two cousins, Arthur and Steve Gould, and stayed there until after World War II.
Went to high school in New York City.
Live with my mother for four years.
And then my uncle got a hold of me.
And he said, you have now finished.
You've graduated high school.
The day that I graduated high school, the Korean War broke out.
And he said to me, what are you going to do now?
And I said, I'm going to get a job.
And he said, forget it.
You better go in the military.
Because there is something that you can handle and you're going to do.
And I said, well, there's a war on.
He said, so what?
You can-- you know take care of yourself.
They'll train you.
So I followed my uncle's orders and enlisted in the United States Army in March 19th, 1951.
And after basic training, I found out that he was right, that this was really something that I could latch on to and something that I could succeed.
And you didn't go to Korea?
No.
I went to Germany.
And half the unit was supposed to go to Korea.
And I went and talked to my-- the personnel officer.
By this time, I was a PFC.
And I said, you know, I'm a German linguist.
And I'm regular army.
I volunteered.
I want to go to Germany.
And he laughed.
And he said, I'll tell you what.
If you find someone who-- a unit that will take you that's going to Germany, we will release you.
Well, I had-- they had already trained me as a demolitions person.
And I started looking around.
And I found the 28th Counter Intelligence Company and-- by accident.
Walked in and I said, are you people going to Germany?
Now, these guys are all sitting around in their, in their t-shirts.
So I had no idea what the-- anyone's rank was.
But there-- I remember there was a stocky gentleman smoking a cigar.
And he said, yeah.
We're going to Germany.
And I said, can you use a German linguist?
And they said, are you one?
And I said, yes.
How well?
And I said, I can speak, write, read, and write German.
And they said, OK.
What, what-- who do you belong to?
I told him.
And within a couple of hours, he-- they came and got me.
And they had the papers, so I knew that it was legit.
And I reported in to them.
And I ended up going to Germany.

And then I ended up going back to the same organization I took basic with, the 103rd Engineers, in a little town-- I chose that one-- and went back to a little town called [INAUDIBLE] and-- which was very close to where my grandmother came from.
So we made full circle.
And when we landed at Bremerhaven in November of 1951, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they called me up on the deck.
And they said, help-- you're you're going to tell these, these stevedores which boxes to unload.
You're going to be an, an interpreter for the loadmaster, for the navy loadmaster.
I said, fine.
No problem.
So I started speaking German.
And I thought to myself standing there, and I said, now, you're back in Germany.
You have a choice.

You're either going to have a miserable three years here and hate everyone who walks around here and blame them for everything that went wrong, or you're going to have a good career here and, and enjoy it, and learn, and, and have a good time.
And I decided that, at that time, that I was to enjoy my, my stay in Germany and, and do the best job I possibly could.
Yes.
For the most part, they succumbed in the concentration camp.
My mother's-- my grandmother's brothers, I think three of them died in a concentration camp.
They did not make it out.
They didn't have the money.
Nobody helped them.
And so they did die in Dachau.
I did have an aunt and uncle-- Hans and Gretel [?
Seagel. ?] Hans was Catholic.
Gretel was Jewish.
And they became slave laborers.
Hans was a slave laborer in a salt mine.
And Gretel was a slave laborer in a book bindery.
And they survived the war.
They were on a train just about to go to Theresienstadt when the [?
Third ?] Army liberated them while they were sitting in the train yard in Munich.
And they just let them go home.
And they-- their, their apartment was still there, which I found fascinating.
They were very strange people.
I got there in '51 and-- trusted no one.
They had a dog.
And they treated that dog like it was their child, the only one that they trusted.
They had this huge bar across the door, a big metal bar that they would put in there.
And I said, do people rob?
No, no we, we have to be very careful.
So they really psychologically never-- I mean, they knew me from when I was a little boy.
But they were not normal people, what I would consider normal people.
Because they were so-- had such mistrust of other human beings.
But some-- you know, it's, it's-- after what they went through, that's understandable.
Nobody else on your father's side?
No.
Friedrich, my uncle, he, he left before my-- before we did.
And he was a banker in Frankfurt, came to this country, settled in New Jersey, became a Fuller Brush man.
And he died in 1963.
And didn't-- we didn't see much of him.
He was a bachelor and different, different.
Uh-huh, come together again.
Well, it was, it was fascinating.
Because here I am, now an American soldier, walking around the same town where I really went through quite a traumatic experience as a kid, and having the run of the place.
I mean, we were occupation troops.
We could literally-- we were god walking around.
So it was, it was fascinating.
I had a lot of connection with the, with the police in this little town.
I was the liaison between the German government and the, and the American government.
And when I walked in the police station, everybody stood up.
There's, there's a picture in one of the albums of, of my commanding officer, Colonel Weston, and I, riding in a German car in a parade.
And I thought, isn't this strange? 12 years before, I-- they kicked me out.
Now I'm in a parade, driving down the main street of this little town.
But they knew you are Jewish?
Uh, they may have.
I, I never, I never hid it.
I never broadcast it.
I mean, you know, people would ask me, where did you ever learn to speak German?
Well, one of the, one of the really strange stories was I came out of our mess hall where we-- our dining facility.
And I went over to buy a soda from this little soda stand.
And I started talking to the gentleman behind the, the counter.
And he said to me, you're German, aren't you?
And I said, well, I'm an American.
He said, no, no.
You were born in Germany.
Your German is too good not to be German.
I said, yes.
I was born in Munich.
When did you leave?
And I told him.
Oh, by the way, where-- what did your father do?
And I said, well, my father was in World War One with the 5th Regiment.
Oh, really?
I said, yeah.
Well, my mother had this picture of my father's platoon for years.
I, I still don't know whatever happened to it.
But anyway, he said, what are you doing after-- this evening?
This was lunchtime.
What are you doing this evening?
And I said, I'll be back for lunch, for supper.
And I came back for supper.
And I went to see him.
And he pulled out this huge, long picture.
And he said, this is the 5th Regiment.
And I'm over here.
And I said, and my father is over here.
And he said, you were both in the same regiment.
And I said, yeah.
And he said, I did not know your father.
My father was in the heavy weapons company, K Company.
And he was in one of the line companies.
So they didn't know each other at all.
But this-- it's-- it was amazing.
So then he-- then I said, well, tell me.
What did you do in World War II?
And he said, you're going to hate me.
I said, no.
I'm-- been over here.
I was part of the denazification also.
I said, I don't hate anyone.
There's no-- I don't, you know.
He said, well, I was a colonel in the Waffen-SS.
And I said, that's interesting.
He said, he said, yeah.
And we let it go at that.
I didn't want to, I didn't want to ask him.
Because I didn't want to know.
And he didn't want to tell me.
Because you know-- and I never talked to him again.
I mean, we-- it isn't that we became friends.
I, I saw him.
And I may have said hi, but that's about it.
We never had another discussion.
But it was just, to me, it was just ironic that here this guy was, selling soda.
And he was a colonel in the Waffen-SS.
Well, when I got overseas, they were still doing denazification.
In other words, that's sort of a weeding out process.
We would-- they would bring people to us.
And the first thing I'd do was make them strip to the waist and check their left armpit-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tattoos, left armpit blood types, SS.
Anyone who had that, we sent them to Frankfurt for further interrogation and so on.
So I was the-- in the preliminaries.
I was at the very beginning, you know, the first echelon, if you will, of weeding out people.
And those that were not in the SS, we checked the, the files to see if they were members of the party, the, the Nazi party.
And if they were, find out exactly what their role was.
In most cases, they just joined just to preserve their job and that type of thing.
I mean, it wasn't-- once in a while, we came across some SS men.
The town where I was was did have a concentration camp, slave labor camp.
They were mostly Poles.
And they were still there when we got there.
It was now a DP camp.
And so that became under my jurisdiction.
Because I not only was a linguist, but I was also part of the military government.
And I had to, more or less, maintain it.
But I really didn't have that much to do.
I had just to make sure that the people had enough to eat, that, that laws and rules and regulations were obeyed, and that the German police did not infringe upon their, upon their rights.
I did get a call once from the chief of police saying to me that he needed a couple of our men and some trucks.
And I said, for what?
He says, well, we've unearthed a mass grave site.
And we need your people to pull those out.
And I hit the roof.
And I said, you have got to be kidding.
Your people are going to pull them out.
Your trucks are going to transport them.
And you're going to give them a decent burial.
I did go out and check on them.
And they did have something like 38 Poles that had been executed.
So I did get a chance to see that.
And one of the early things I did was to go to visit Dachau.
And I did see relatives' names in their book.
They have a book.
They had a book there.
And I did I go see names there, so [SIGHS] it was tough.
Very difficult, you know, very difficult time.
And I'm sure it's left its mark on me, going through all that.
I don't know.
But I-- well, I don't want to get into that.
I really enjoyed my job.
I had a, I had a tremendously good commanding officer who really took care of me.
And he would say, go take a three-day pass.
Come on, Freund.
You need to get away from this nonsense.
I enjoy what I was doing, because I felt I was bringing back Germany into the threshold of, of nations, of honorable nations.
So I felt that it was a worthwhile job.
I also felt that I owed this country a tremendous amount that I can-- a debt.
I could never repay them.
And that's one of the reasons why I stayed in, in, in the-- well, the regular-- why I joined the regular army and stayed in the reserve program.
And I would be on active duty for 110, 120, sometimes 130 days.
I enjoyed it.
Plus I really felt I was doing something worthwhile.
And in Germany, I did a lot of things for the US government that I thought were absolutely necessary to bring this country back into the, into the fold.
